title: Jeremiah 31
subtitle: The New Covenant

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — New Covenant Prophecy

sections.header: Verses 1-22 -- "I Have Loved You with an Everlasting Love"

heb.paragraph: "I have loved you with an everlasting love ('ahavat 'olam); I have drawn you with unfailing kindness" (v.3). The phrase combines 'ahava (love) with 'olam (eternal/everlasting) -- divine love that has no beginning and no end. This is not sentimental affection but covenant commitment that persists through judgment, exile, and apparent abandonment.

"Rachel weeping for her children" (v.15) -- the matriarch of the northern tribes grieves at Ramah as her descendants march into exile. Matthew 2:18 applies this verse to the slaughter of the innocents, reading Rachel's tears as a recurring motif of innocent suffering that is ultimately comforted by divine redemption.

refs.note: Matthew quotes Jeremiah 31:15 for the slaughter of the innocents -- Rachel's tears continue wherever innocent children suffer.
"For a brief moment I abandoned you, but with great compassion I will bring you back" -- the same everlasting love theology.
"Your grief will turn to joy" -- Jesus promises the same reversal Jeremiah envisions.

notes.note: "The people who survive the sword will find favor in the wilderness." Grace meets the survivors of catastrophe. The wilderness is no longer a place of judgment but of new encounter with God.
"I am Israel's father, and Ephraim is my firstborn son." The northern kingdom -- exiled since 722 BC -- is still claimed by God as firstborn. Exile has not dissolved the relationship.
Rachel weeping at Ramah: the deportation route passed through Ramah, near Rachel's tomb. The comfort is specific: "Your children will return."
"Is not Ephraim my dear son, the child in whom I delight?" God's "inner parts are stirred" (hamu me'ay) for Ephraim -- visceral, parental compassion.

Calvin makes this verse a pillar of election theology: "God's love precedes all human response. We love because he first loved us. The 'everlasting love' began before creation and extends beyond history."
Calvin reads Rachel's weeping as representative of all parental grief: "God honors the tears of mothers. The comfort that follows does not trivialize the loss but transforms it."

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Ramah was a town about five miles north of Jerusalem, traditionally associated with Rachel's burial (1 Sam 10:2). It was also a staging area for deportees (Jer 40:1).
"A woman will surround a man" -- the Hebrew is cryptic. Interpretations range from military reversal to sexual initiative to the virgin birth. The ambiguity may be intentional.

lundbom.source: Jack R. Lundbom, Jeremiah, Anchor Bible (3 vols., 1999–2004) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lundbom structures this section as a progressive restoration: land (vv.2-6), people (vv.7-14), grief-to-comfort (vv.15-17), repentance (vv.18-20), and new creation (vv.21-22). Each layer builds toward the climactic new covenant oracle. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

brueggemann.source: Walter Brueggemann, Jeremiah, ITC (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Brueggemann calls "everlasting love" the "theological ground zero of the Book of Consolation." Everything that follows flows from the simple claim that God's love precedes and survives every catastrophe. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

hist.context: The first half of chapter 31 is a cascade of restoration promises: rebuilt cities, replanted vineyards, reunited north and south, joy replacing mourning. The Rachel-weeping passage (vv.15-17) is a moment of raw grief within the celebration -- God does not deny the suffering but promises that "they will return from the land of the enemy" and "there is hope for your descendants." The famous "new thing" of v.22 -- "a woman will surround a man" -- has provoked endless interpretation.

sections.header: Verses 23-40 -- "I Will Make a New Covenant"

heb.paragraph: "I will make a new covenant (berit hadasha) with the people of Israel and with the people of Judah" (v.31). This is the only occurrence of the phrase "new covenant" in the Hebrew Bible. The newness is not in the covenant partners or the ethical content but in the mechanism: God will write the law "on their hearts" ('al-libbam). External inscription on stone is replaced by internal transformation of the person. The failure was not in the law but in the people's capacity to obey it. The new covenant solves the anthropological problem.

"I will forgive (salaḥ) their wickedness and will remember their sins no more" (v.34). The verb salaḥ is used exclusively of divine forgiveness -- only God can salaḥ. The promise of complete forgiveness is the foundation of the new covenant: the knowledge of God becomes universal because the barrier of sin has been permanently removed.

refs.note: The full quotation of Jeremiah 31:31-34 -- the longest OT citation in the NT. The author of Hebrews uses it to argue that the old covenant is "obsolete" (Heb 8:13).
"This cup is the new covenant in my blood" -- Jesus identifies his death as the inauguration of Jeremiah's new covenant. The Last Supper is the fulfillment event.
Paul contrasts "tablets of stone" with "tablets of human hearts" -- directly developing Jeremiah's contrast between external and internal inscription.
Ezekiel's parallel promise: "I will give you a new heart and put a new spirit in you." The heart transplant metaphor complements Jeremiah's inscription metaphor.

notes.note: "New covenant" -- the only use of this exact phrase in the OT. The Sinai covenant was not defective in its terms but in the people's capacity to obey. The new covenant addresses the root problem: the human heart.

"Not like the covenant I made with their ancestors" -- the old covenant was breakable because it depended on human obedience. The new covenant is unbreakable because it depends on divine transformation.

"I will put my law in their minds and write it on their hearts." The law is not changed but internalized. What was external command becomes internal nature. The heart of stone becomes a heart of flesh (Ezek 36:26).

"No longer will they teach their neighbor" -- not the elimination of teaching but the universality of divine knowledge. Every member of the covenant community will know God directly. "I will forgive their wickedness" provides the basis: sin no longer blocks the relationship.
The guarantee: as long as the sun, moon, and stars endure, Israel will remain a nation. The new covenant is as permanent as creation itself.

Calvin identifies four marks of the new covenant: "First, the law written on hearts -- internal transformation. Second, the knowledge of God without intermediaries. Third, universality -- from least to greatest. Fourth, complete forgiveness as the foundation of all the rest. Without forgiveness, none of the other marks are possible."

Calvin insists that the "newness" is not in the moral content (the law remains) but in the mode of implementation: "God does not change what he requires but changes the people who receive the requirement. The law is the same; the heart is new."

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase berit hadasha ("new covenant") occurs only here in the Hebrew Bible. The LXX translates it as diatheke kaine, which becomes the standard NT term and eventually the title of the "New Testament" itself.

The verb yada' ("know") in "they will all know me" is the same intimate, relational knowledge that has been the missing element throughout Jeremiah (2:8, 4:22, 9:3, 24). What was demanded and denied is now promised and given.

lundbom.source: Jack R. Lundbom, Jeremiah, Anchor Bible (3 vols., 1999–2004) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lundbom calls this "the most important passage in the book and arguably in the entire Hebrew Bible for subsequent Christian theology." The four clauses form a carefully structured announcement: (1) internalization of Torah, (2) covenant formula renewed, (3) universal knowledge, (4) complete forgiveness. Each clause addresses a specific failure documented in chapters 1-29.

brueggemann.source: Walter Brueggemann, Jeremiah, ITC (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Brueggemann reads the new covenant as "the prophetic answer to the anthropological crisis of the heart." The deceitful heart (17:9) that cannot change itself (13:23) will be transformed by divine inscription. What was diagnosed as incurable (30:12) is now promised as healed. The new covenant is not a new set of rules but a new kind of person.

hist.context: Jeremiah 31:31-34 is the theological summit of the Hebrew Bible and the most important OT text for Christian theology. Hebrews 8:8-12 quotes it in full -- the longest OT quotation in the NT -- to argue that Christ inaugurates the new covenant that Jeremiah prophesied. The four features of the new covenant are: (1) God's law written on hearts, not stone; (2) unmediated knowledge of God ("they will all know me"); (3) a universal covenant community ("from the least to the greatest"); (4) complete forgiveness of sins. Jesus explicitly invokes this passage at the Last Supper: "This cup is the new covenant in my blood" (Luke 22:20). The chapter closes with creation theology: the permanence of the new covenant is guaranteed by the permanence of the created order.

rows.label: vv.38-40
rows.text: Jerusalem Rebuilt: Holy to the LORD

lit.note: The theological summit of the Hebrew Bible. Everything in Jeremiah -- the broken covenant, the deceitful heart, the inability to change, the failed institutions -- finds its answer in the new covenant (vv.31-34). The structure moves from restoration promises to the central oracle to creation guarantee.

themes.note: Covenant and Mercy both peak at 10 -- the new covenant is the supreme expression of both. This is the only chapter in Jeremiah where Mercy matches Judgment's usual dominance.

hebtext.note: "I have loved you with an everlasting love ('ahavat 'olam); I have drawn you with unfailing kindness" (v.3). The phrase combines 'ahava (love) with 'olam (eternal/everlasting) -- divine love that has no beginning and no end. This is not sentimental affection but covenant commitment that persists through judgment, exile, and apparent abandonment.

"I will make a new covenant (berit hadasha) with the people of Israel and with the people of Judah" (v.31). This is the only occurrence of the phrase "new covenant" in the Hebrew Bible. The newness is not in the covenant partners or the ethical content but in the mechanism: God will write the law "on their hearts" ('al-libbam). External inscription on stone is replaced by internal transformation of the person. The failure was not in the law but in the people's capacity to obey it. The new covenant solves the anthropological problem.

The promise of complete forgiveness is the foundation of the new covenant: the knowledge of God becomes universal because the barrier of sin has been permanently removed.

trans.title: Comparing translations in The New Covenant

translations.text: See, I will bring them from the land of the north and gather them from the ends of the earth. Among them will be the blind and the lame, expectant mothers and women in labor; a great throng will return.

Behold, I will bring them from the north country and gather them from the farthest parts of the earth, among them the blind and the lame, the pregnant woman and she who is in labor, together; a great company, they shall return here.
I will build you up again, and you, Virgin Israel, will be rebuilt. Again you will take up your timbrels and go out to dance with the joyful.
Again I will build you, and you shall be built, O virgin Israel! Again you shall adorn yourself with tambourines and shall go forth in the dance of the merrymakers.

Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads The New Covenant within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.

thread.text: Matthew quotes Jeremiah 31:15 for the slaughter of the innocents -- Rachel's tears continue wherever innocent children suffer.
"For a brief moment I abandoned you, but with great compassion I will bring you back" -- the same everlasting love theology.
"Your grief will turn to joy" -- Jesus promises the same reversal Jeremiah envisions.
The full quotation of Jeremiah 31:31-34 -- the longest OT citation in the NT. The author of Hebrews uses it to argue that the old covenant is "obsolete" (Heb 8:13).
"This cup is the new covenant in my blood" -- Jesus identifies his death as the inauguration of Jeremiah's new covenant. The Last Supper is the fulfillment event.
Paul contrasts "tablets of stone" with "tablets of human hearts" -- directly developing Jeremiah's contrast between external and internal inscription.

coaching.tip: 'I have loved you with an everlasting love; I have drawn you with unfailing kindness' (v. 3). The Hebrew hesed ('unfailing kindness') is the covenant-loyalty word — love that persists because of commitment, not because of the beloved's merit. Jeremiah grounds restoration not in Israel's repentance but in God's character. The initiative is entirely divine.

'I will make a new covenant' (v. 31). This is the only Old Testament passage that uses the phrase 'new covenant.' Jesus quotes it at the Last Supper (Luke 22:20); the book of Hebrews builds its entire argument on it (Hebrews 8:8–12). The old covenant was written on stone; the new one on hearts. Jeremiah envisions internalized obedience — not law abolished but law relocated. The entire New Testament gets its name from these verses.

prayer_prompt: As God declares 'I will make a new covenant — I will put my law in their minds and write it on their hearts,' consider asking God whether His law feels like an external obligation or an internal desire — and what would shift it.
